speaker: interviewer
text: You were on your way to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And he took me to a area where they mobilizing those young people. They had to sterilize my clothes and give me documents, and included me into transport with Polish people. The train was-- la-- the train ride lasted a whole night because they kept stopping. And finally I arrived Germany, Dusseldorf. And from there, I took a bus and arrived to Witzhelden, Germany, where [GERMAN] mother lived. That was their summer home, but now because of the bombs they lived there permanently. And I came in and I said that I have regards from their son. And she cried and welcomed-- wanted to know all the details. And I was to clean the house. There's a big-- it's a villa with many rooms. To clean and help with the cooking. It was a very beautiful surrounding. They were very rich people. They had ships and yachts and things. But during-- due to the danger in Cologne, they lived there. I told them, bringing regards from her son from the front line. She was in tears and grateful, and they liked me. And then what happened, I worked there. And it was beautiful. And they were very kind to me. And then something happened, that from their authorities from the nearby town, they felt that me working at the household, that's not contributing to Germany. I should work in a factory. They need--

speaker: interviewee
text: So because they did not like them-- because they had a chip on their shoulder because they were the rich, to hit the rich, there was somebody who was a Nazi. And he reported that I should go work in a factory, [GERMAN], near Cologne.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to bid goodbye, and her daughter, Dorothy, took me to-- to-- I was afraid that I-- if I'm going to be in-- at camp with Polish girl, I have to be more watchful because they may sense that I'm Jewish. But the Germans, they don't know the difference between a Jewish girl or Christian. It was a factory. Before the war, it was a textile. They were manufacturing beautiful materials. But this time, they converted it to the military. And they were producing-- it's like these-- the-- the soldiers are wearing, it's like with leaves for the-- like for the parachute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Camouflage. Oh, parachute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Camo-- the camouflage. If-- if a soldier lay down wearing that poncho, you thought they are leaves. And that factory constantly had air-- air raids because the-- the roof of the factory had water. It had-- [DRINKS] --had glass.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if there is an explosion, the impact-- in fact, at one time a bomb fell in a nearby facility, and from the impact all that glass came down. Whenever there was an alarm, the Germans on the bicycle went to a bunker to hide. And they said, it's so powerful that even a-- if a bomb would fall directly, it wouldn't penetrate.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we foreigners were not allowed. To crawl under the machinery would crush us. So we ran into the chimney. Factory chimney had like a door open. The Polish boys and girls, we were separate barracks, ran to the chimney. At one time, we were all in the chimney. We just made it. Bombs were falling. And when the alarm sign that it's over, we opened that door. There was a bomb landed right in front of the entrance of that chimney. It was a dud. It did not explode. Had it explode, we would have been torn to pieces and dead, because it was a powerful bomb. They had experts coming to dismantle it. And they cautiously let us pass through. And I was thinking co-- conceitedly, God knows that I'm here and he wants me to survive. That's why that bomb didn't explode-- explode. Because I constantly had conversations with God.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I doubted. Does he exist? If such thing. But I had to dismiss it, because without that belief that God is going to save me, I would be lost completely. I inherited that belief in God.

speaker: interviewee
text: The boys were-- and the girls were friendly. And they played the accordion and they danced in that dining area. And when-- whenever there was an alarm, it was near the factory, part of the fac-- we ran to the chimney. Because winter came and it was cold, I shared a bed with-- up a bunk. I was in up-- to have more blankets, we shared together with a girl, Maria. She was from some town in Poland. And she said to me, Paulina, in your dream, you cry and you call out, and it breaks my heart. Then I was afraid. I cannot control my dreams. I may call out in Yiddish something. So I say-- I went back on the upper bunks, she shouldn't-- and I was afraid to sleep, if my dreams control me. But otherwise, the girls, I didn't have-- winter came, I didn't have any clothes when I ran. So she gave my sweater. They were very nice. But I used to hear anti-- anti-Semitic remarks about the Jews, about things. But with them, I was Christian. Christmas came. And the Richters, where I worked, invited me to their house. So I went. And they invited me simply so that I should clean up the kitchen and do the dishes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was Christmas of '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then I came back to the-- to the camp, stayed with them. And then the bombs being this near Cologne. Day and night, they were bombing. And [PAUSES] we heard rumors that they already on the Rhine, on the river Rhine. And day and night, there were bombs and we ran to the chimney. And then one time-- at one time, we knew that because of the factory and so, they're going to bomb. We're going to die. So I and another girl, we wal-- went back at night. We walked back to Witzhelden. There was a girl there, a guard and a girl that she was kind. We didn't go to the Richters. We went because she had somebody else already. And we stayed there at the outskirts of the town for safety. And then the war was over. The English and Americans occupied. I was-- when they took me away and when the factory was sort of fell apart because they didn't have coal, so we were at loose ends. And the one in charge left. So you are on your own. That's when I went to the Witzhelden. And as the war lingered on, I had a job at-- at some-- at a woman, [INAUDIBLE]. She was a dentist. I stayed. And the girl Maria that went with me also found a job. And in-- in her house, we were hiding in the cellar. And she would say, God knows what our Fuehrer's doing. He meant well for us. It just didn't turn out. And when there all I walked into Witzhelden, that small town-- that small town there, from the window we saw German soldiers throwing off in a cemetery, throwing off their uniforms. And the Germans running out and giving them civilian clothes, that they shouldn't be taken prisoner. And then when I looked out from the garage door, I saw German soldiers and I like-- maybe American or-- it was American or English, on the-- with their rifles, leading the Germans hands up, leading them through the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: My woman, the lady that I worked for, she cried and wept because her husband was in the army, too. And she embraced me, Paula, Fraulein Paula, what's going to be? How different were our tears.

speaker: interviewee
text: I cried because it was too late for my parents, too late for Muno, too late for Zigi, too late for Laura, for Rosa. They dreamed-- my father used to say, I just want to see the Germany capitulated, and then I may die. They didn't see that moment where the Germans, with their hands up. And they were-- then they placed an order-- there was in order that the Germans from this side have to cross the road and take a co-- take up-- go into their neighbor's house. They have to leave their homes because the American, the Allies, they will take over those apartments. They need quarters to stay. Then she, in the last minute, she wanted to get her preserves and get things. So I hear a German-- American soldier say, [SPEAKING YIDDISH.] I realized he was an American soldier speaking Yiddish. But it was too soon for me to reveal myself, who I am. I know he's a Yiddish, a Jewish soldier. And the Germans, we went on across-- across the road, stay for safety. And they occupied those quarters, the private houses and our house. And they were from the-- watching from the window the tro-- the Allied troops marching in. And they were admiring their spunk, the way they're full of life. Their soldiers were walking with sores, with torn shoes, torn up, not shaved, because they were on the retreat. To me, that was music. And they were crying. And one woman says, oh, God knows what our Fuehrer's doing. He meant well for us. But it didn't turn out. Even though the Americans were coming in, even though it-- they could be retreat and it's dangerous, I could not resist anymore. When she cried, the Fuehrer meant well, I cried out, if Fuehrer wanted to rob and kill the whole world and feed their own Germans at the expense of people-- I said, I'm Jewish. My parents, my family were killed, because he wanted [GERMAN]. And Mrs. [INAUDIBLE], this dentist, she was hysterical afraid that I'm going to point her out to the American. I said, no. The Americans are not going to kill. They are liberators. They are not going to do what you did to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then that night the soldiers were moving in from the windows. We were seeing them coming in. And I thought, perhaps I spoke too soon. Should they retreat? I should have held my mouth. But when she was sorry for Hitler-- oh, God knows what our Fuerher's doing. Then I couldn't hold it back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as time went on, the troops were-- the more I saw them coming, the more I felt that it's not a temporary occupation. They came. But it was too soon to reveal myself to the Americans. Then I went-- I left and I went to Frankfurt, which was nearby. Frankfurt am Main, where finally maybe there's some Jewish survivors and so on. And there was an office where people, whoever survived Germans-- not-- not me-- whoever survived, they had a committee and gathering. And not Frankfurt. Cologne, I'm sorry. This was Cologne, because it was-- Frankfurt was-- was not. This was for the German Jews in Cologne. So some Germans who survived came out of hiding. They had some German friends who they paid them, or out of friendship, and they told me that I'm a Polish Jew. They have so much misery that they don't know what to do first for the survivors. But they know that in Frankfurt am Main, there is a DP camp for Polish Jews, that I should go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took the train and went to Frankfurt am Main. [INAUDIBLE] was the area where Jews kept coming. And I stayed there. And I got a job with the registration because every time they registered the population, somebody walking there on the camp-- the UNRRA was providing American-- providing ration cards for them. There was a kitchen. It was freedom. The UNRRA was providing all that. And there was a listing of survivors, that whoever looks could see. Very often I saw brothers finding a sister. And I said, wouldn't it be a wonderful way to find somebody? But where would my family be in Germany? Not.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked at the registration. And then there was a registration-- I registered. Mrs. Roosevelt came to visit the DP camps with a whole entourage of people. And that camp, there was already erected a statue for the six million victims. And in front of that statue, she spoke to us that now we have a chance to go, if we want to go to America. There's going to be immigration. And very kindly spoke to us. There was no Pal-- there was still Palestine and the illegal people-- trucks were turned back. So I thought, I'll go to America. And I was thinking, I don't know if my brothers are alive. But if they are alive, someday we'll find them. And there was a registration to America. But there was a German quarter, not a Polish quarter. And the people at the office at the administration said I should put myself in the German quarter. And suddenly, we knew that whoever from my family survives, we'll find ourself through my uncle who's in Switzerland. It's a neutral country. Through him, my Uncle Lazar. So I wrote to Switzerland. And my uncle moved from Zurich to Lugano, so it was another-- and it took time till they found him. One time, there was on the board announcing that there's mail for me. And my uncle-- I received a letter from my uncle. He knew where I was. That my brother, Muno, Max, is alive. He's in Austria. After the liberation, he got into Austria. It's a long story. And he let him know. And if I-- while I worked for the-- for the American in the office, for the registration, I obtained a room by a German family, a clean, nice room. For products, we would receive rations. I gave and I stayed there. I'm in my room, and the German lady says, there's somebody to see you. And she hands me a photograph. And I look at it. It was a recent picture of my brother, where-- from-- he aged. You saw sadness and pain in him. I remember him a carefree boy. I said, this is my brother. He prepared me for the shock, and he appeared, my brother. He said, my brother, Zigmund, was shot. Did not survive. The-- they were running. He was killed. I was hurt because I got only half of the bargain, but at least I had my brother. And he was telling me that my parents were taken to Belzec. This was a-- a liquidation camp, not a work camp. And we-- and we were happy with each other. And then there was res-- a registration to go to America. And I was already by the CIA, already questioned and interviewed and already had the papers. So I cried. If my brother has to go through that procedure, I'm going to go to Bremen, to the port, where from the ship leaves. was the ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they said, you go. Your brother will register. I said, I'm afraid that we get lost again. I would-- can I stay back? And it happened so that I went and I cried and I  begged, and my brother registered. And in time, they united us and we were able to go to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Bremen. It's a port. And on a ship, they kept us quarantined there. And then a ship, a ship-- it was a military converted. We boarded the ship. And we were leaving Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thinking, why would it-- was it too late for Laura, too late for Rosa, too late for them? My father wanted to live only for that moment, to see German capitulate. But I was grateful I had my brother. It was beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a kitchen there with foods. And we-- they served us foods, cafeteria type. And we would eat things that we hadn't had in years. And as we moved up with the ship further, into the sea, many people were seasick, but lying there sick. My brother and I, we didn't get seasick. We ate everything. And there was such a feeling, leaving. I felt I'll never go back to Europe that's soaked with blood. And we appro-- for 10 days. The trip was for 10 days. And some people, there were not just people from German Jews and from Beligum, from other countries that survived. They were singing. They formed a group, a theater group. It was like life. And then we approached New York, and they told us to come out, see the Statue of Liberty. It was a door-- [CRYING] and we-- we got up out on the deck. And we saw the Statue of Liberty with that torch. Everybody cried.

speaker: interviewee
text: I cried. Why couldn't Zigmund live? Why couldn't Laura or Rosa? Why couldn't they be-- go to this better life? It's too late for them. The pain is too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES] We arrived to New York. And they took us-- there was from the Joint Distribution Committee, like the highest, they took us from the ship to a place, to a hotel on 103rd Street and Broadway. Now it's a different neighborhood, but at that time it was a Jewish neighborhood. And they had rooms ready, this-- this charitable Jewish committees. They knew the ship is arriving. And my brother had-- it was boys with boys, and girl-- we had a room. And there was a kitchen. They serve us food and we ate like pigs because we ate all the things that we didn't have. And then my brother and I, we said, you know, I remember my mother had cousins in Brooklyn. Axael, it was A-X-A-E-L, Axael. [INAUDIBLE] was their name. Maybe we'll call. We'll find them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't speak English, but the names we could read because it's English. It's like-- so Axael, there were maybe 50 Axaels, and we kept calling. We were-- had free use of the telephone, they let us use. And my mother's name was Charlotte. Are you my relative? Until we found somebody, a cousin, Axael, who said, oh my God. Charlotte [YIDDISH], was the Yiddish, my mother's name. [YIDDISH] [GERMAN]? Children of-- children. They're coming to see us at the hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it came, my cousin and his wife. And he said he'll describe himself. He wears like a pink shirt, a sport shirt. And my brother and I, we waited and we saw. And they came. We recognized them. Embraced us. And we-- we were gregarious. We wanted to tell them everything what was done to us. And they-- they thought, perhaps, that we talk too much. But we had to. They invited us to their home. And all the other relatives, younger people who didn't-- my cousin knew my mother in her youth, and so, but they did not know. Introduced us. The [YIDDISH]. And whatever I had of clothes, they felt maybe I'm contaminated. Tore off me, even thought I was not contaminated. And they took me, bought me clothes. Whoever didn't have relatives stayed on at the hotel until they find them something. And my cousin took us. And my cousin said, like that. You are a girl. You need a home. Your brother can get a furnished room, we'll get him. He'll find a job. And he-- I-- so-- they-- she took me and bought me dresses, which were too long.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Europe, we-- they taught it in school, sewing and brocading and stitching, we knew. So I was sitting shortening. So she said, you know how to sew? You could be a finisher. You could have a job. So she went, there was a Crawford clothing store. She went. She said, this is a [YIDDISH], a European, of cour-- she just came. And so-- and they were sympathetic. Maybe they were not impressed with my ability, but everybody wanted to contribute to the survivors. And everybody wanted to tell the stories, what we went through. And the first thing that everybody asked, were you raped? Were you raped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they thought that is the-- I was never raped. But I had to like apologize, like maybe they don't believe me. That was not the case. But that was the main concern, other things. But they listened intently. And then I realized I'm talking too much. They cannot grasp it. It seems so unreal that they may think I'm telling fibs, just dramatizing it. And when you don't get the listener who really absorbs it, then you don't talk anymore. You don't want to tell, because they cannot imagine. They cannot understand. Just like we in Germany, the Jews-- my aunts came and told us. We still thought that does not apply to us. That happens in Germany, not to us. Until you live it, you know it. And I stayed with my cousin. And my brother got a job. Because my father was in the tailoring trade, my brother knew how to sew. Sometimes he helped. He got a job at a company. I got a job at Crawford. And I already made $30 a week. And my cousin was very good to me. It was my mother's cousin. He was the cousin. She was the wife. She had no children. Loving. And washed my hair and nourished me, stuffed in me food. And I had a job. And I went enrolled to night school to learn English. And at night, those times in 1946, we were not afraid to go at night. She was in Brooklyn. And near Prospect Avenue was a night school for refugees. I walked at night and coming home late, my heels clicking, I saw my shadow. I never thought somebody's going to grab me, somebody's going to kill me. I learned English from its foundation. And when I learned English, I felt I could look for better jobs. And so-- and my cousin-- my brother would come every-- for Sabbath every Friday night, stay over the weekend. He found a job. He worked. And he had a furnished room, supported himself. My relatives bought me dresses and suits and shoes. And they were very good to me.

words.text: You don't want to tell, because

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but I was gregarious. I just wanted to tell them everything that happened. Sometimes I felt, they think I talk too much. But isn't it my duty to tell what was done to my family? No matter how you could be sympathetic, nobody, nobody in this world could grasp the magnitude of this tragedy, what took place.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- after awhile, they don't want to expose upon themself that pain. They don't want to hear it. It's like there are some survivors who for years did not tell, because it was too painful for them to talk. I personally felt-- my father gave us the document. They were starving when they gave us the document so that somebody should survive to tell. Then I have to tell what was done to them. I am the survivor. If they could endure being gassed, then I should endure telling what was done to us. And then after night school, I loved life in America. People were very kind and generous. [PAUSES] And [PAUSES] I went-- being at night school. My brother went to night school, my Muno. And he met his wife at night school. And then I went to Sabina's wedding. My husband-- I-- and my husband, coincidentally, he was the best man. And he noticed me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was on two.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He noticed me. He asked me to dance. Because he was tall, he was pulling up my blouse, pulling out my dress. I was annoyed. And when he asked me for the telephone, I figure I'll be polite. But I just was not impressed. But as it turned out, he persisted. He pursued me. He was also a survivor. He said-- were 12 brothers. Four survived. But he was in Russia. He ran to Russia. He was not on the German occupation, which was good for him. His brothers remained on the Germans and died. And like I was telling you, we could have lived if we would have ran to Russia at that time. But we knew. And he came to my Aunt Sarah, who was so good to me when she gave me a home. And she said, if you meet somebody, don't meet in the street. Bring to the house. I was fortunate. She made meals for him. [PAUSES] And then after some time, he proposed to me. And I felt I wanted a home and children. And he's hardworking. He's going to be good to me. So I got married. And my husband worked very hard, provided for me. I raised my children the best I can, but sometimes [PAUSES] the consequence of the concentration camp was paid. Outwardly, I'm uplifting and talking. And take pride in my appearance and my home and my children. But I cannot control my subconscious. Nightmares, dreams. Later years, they are more-- less frequent. But in my dreams, I am-- I see I'm running, hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: I constantly-- constant-- constantly in danger. Cons--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES] I wake up from my dream that I'm hiding in a cellar, where-- where they-- where I see a rifle thrust in. They're going to shoot me. And I beg, don't shoot me. Don't kill me. And I'm begging for my life. Then I wake up in a sweat. I realize that I'm in America. I'm free. This is only a dream. And I go to-- I used to go to the room and touch my children, just to verify that they are real. [PAUSES] And [PAUSES] there are consequences because the nightmares persist. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I always feel that they're shooting after me. They're killing me. Where should I run? Where should I hide? Like a cornered animal. They're coming to get me. The gestapo's here. And I'm begging for my life. And it-- this repeats constantly.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES] But-- but I cannot control my-- my dreams. I see-- I see my parents in my dreams. I see my parents, where they were taken to the gas chambers. And I go with them. And I'm talking, when I think-- I think that I see my father. And I'm sure that in the last-- with-- within his last breath, he cried out, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Calling to God before he-- his last breath. And I think of my-- and the bodies twisting, and I'm with them. I'm choking with them after that nightmare. Then I wake my-- wake up and bring myself to reality, because I am in America. I'm safe. But those dreams keep coming back. There is no escape.
